Given: $C_P = 3{,}00{,}000$, $E_P = 2.4$ QALYs; $C_Q = 1{,}20{,}000$, $E_Q = 1.6$ QALYs; $\lambda = 1{,}50{,}000$/QALY.

**Step 1 — Compute incremental cost and effect:**

$$
\Delta C = 3{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}80{,}000
$$

$$
\Delta E = 2.4 - 1.6 = 0.8 \text{ QALYs}
$$

— **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Compute ICER:**

$$
\text{ICER} = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{0.8} = \text{₹}2{,}25{,}000 \text{ per QALY}
$$

— **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Compare with WTP threshold:**

$$
\text{ICER} = ₹2{,}25{,}000 > \lambda = ₹1{,}50{,}000
$$

— **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Decision:** Strategy P is **NOT cost-effective** at the current WTP threshold. — **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — NMB cross-check:**

$$
\text{NMB}_P = (1{,}50{,}000)(2.4) - 3{,}00{,}000 = 60{,}000
$$

$$
\text{NMB}_Q = (1{,}50{,}000)(1.6) - 1{,}20{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000
$$

Since $\text{NMB}_Q > \text{NMB}_P$, adopt Strategy Q. — **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define DALY. Derive its mathematical expression and explain YLL and YLD components with one healthcare example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A new TB vaccination programme targets a population of 10 lakh. Estimated outcomes:
- Prevents 5,000 deaths; residual life expectancy at death = 30 years
- Averts 50,000 disability cases; avg disability weight DW = 0.2, duration = 2 years
- Discount rate $\delta$ = 3%, age of onset = 40 years

Calculate the DALYs averted. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution B(a):**

**Definition (1 mark):** DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year) is a valued target variable that quantifies the burden of disease as the sum of years of life lost due to premature mortality (YLL) and years lived with disability (YLD).

**Mathematical derivation (3 marks):**

$$
\text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD}
$$

$$
\text{YLL} = N \cdot L
$$

where $N$ = number of deaths, $L$ = residual life expectancy at age of death.

$$
\text{YLD} = I \cdot DW \cdot L
$$

where $I$ = incident cases, $DW$ = disability weight $\in [0,1]$, $L$ = duration of disability.

**Examples (3 marks):**
- **YLL example:** 1,000 deaths from road accidents at age 25 with $L=60$ years → YLL = 60,000 person-years.
- **YLD example:** 500 stroke cases with DW = 0.5 lasting 5 years → YLD = 1,250 person-years.

---

**Model Solution B(b):**

Given: $N = 5{,}000$, $L_{YLL} = 30$ years; $I = 50{,}000$, $DW = 0.2$, $L_{YLD} = 2$ years; $\delta = 0.03$, $a = 40$.

**Step 1 — YLL (undiscounted):**

$$
\text{YLL} = 5{,}000 \times 30 = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ person-years}
$$ — **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Discounted YLL:**

$$
\text{YLL}_{disc} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{(1.03)^{40}}
$$

Computing $(1.03)^{40} \approx 3.262$:

$$
\text{YLL}_{disc} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{3.262} \approx 45{,}985 \text{ person-years}
$$

— **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — YLD (undiscounted):**

$$
\text{YLD} = 50{,}000 \times 0.2 \times 2 = 20{,}000 \text{ person-years}
$$ — **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Discounted YLD (midpoint approximation):**

$$
\text{YLD}_{disc} = \frac{20{,}000}{(1.03)^{40 + 1}} = \frac{20{,}000}{3.360} \approx 5{,}952 \text{ person-years}
$$

— **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Total DALYs averted:**

$$
\text{DALY}_{averted} = 45{,}985 + 5{,}952 = 51{,}937 \text{ person-years}
$$

— **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to discount** both YLL and YLD — KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** if discount rate is omitted in long derivations.
> 2. **Sign confusion in NMB:** Always subtract cost, not add. $\text{NMB} = \lambda E - C$.
> 3. **Confusing ICER direction:** $\Delta C$ and $\Delta E$ must reference the **new intervention minus comparator**, not the reverse.
> 4. **DW range error:** Disability weight is bounded $\in [0, 1]$; values outside this range automatically lose marks.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Reporting ICER as "₹/QALY" without the per-QALY suffix — **half-mark penalty** typically applied.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **valued target variable** converts raw clinical outcomes into commensurable units (₹, QALY, DALY).
- ✅ **QALY** = $\sum u_i \cdot \Delta t_i$; utility scale is **0 to 1**.
- ✅ **DALY** = YLL + YLD = $N \cdot L + I \cdot DW \cdot L$; quantifies **disease burden**.
- ✅ **ICER** = $\Delta C / \Delta E$; decision rule: adopt if ICER $\leq \lambda$.
- ✅ **NMB** = $\lambda \cdot E - C$; adopt if $\Delta \text{NMB} > 0$ (preferred over ICER for statistical inference).
- ✅ WHO-standard **discount rate** = **3%**; India's WTP threshold $\lambda \approx$ **₹1,50,000/QALY** (1× GDP per capita).
- ✅ **Disability weight (DW)** $\in [0, 1]$ — values close to 1 indicate severe disability.
- ✅ Always **cross-verify** ICER decisions using NMB to avoid quadrant misinterpretation.
- ✅ Common valued targets in KTU context: **QALY, DALY, ICER, NMB, EMV, EVPI**.
- ✅ In ML pipelines, valued targets are used as **loss function weights** in cost-sensitive learning (e.g., class-weighted logistic regression).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Python: Variables and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT523 - HEALTHCARE ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Python: Variables and Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable (KTU Definition):** A *variable* in Python is a symbolic name (identifier) bound to an object stored in the computer's memory. Unlike statically-typed languages (C, Java), Python variables are **dynamically typed references** — they do not own a fixed data type; instead, they point to objects whose type is determined at runtime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Type Definition:** A *data type* is a classification that specifies the kind of value a Python object can hold and the operations that can be performed on it. Python's built-in (primitive) types include `int`, `float`, `complex`, `bool`, `str`, and `NoneType`, while container types include `list`, `tuple`, `set`, and `dict`.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a Python **variable as a sticky label pasted on a box** rather than the box itself.

- The **box** = the actual object sitting on a shelf in memory (containing the value).
- The **sticky label** = the variable name you write in your code.
- The **shape printed on the box** = the data type of the object.

You can peel the label off one box and stick it onto another box of a different size — that is **dynamic typing**. In a healthcare analytics context, the "box" could hold a patient's heart rate (`int`), then later be reassigned to hold the patient's body temperature (`float`), and then a name (`str`) — all without changing the label.

### 1.3 Standard KTU Metrics and Reserved Terminology

| Term | KTU Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Identifier** | A user-defined name for variables, functions, or classes |
| **Object** | Any value stored in memory in Python (everything is an object) |
| **Dynamic Typing** | Type checking happens at **runtime**, not at compile time |
| **Type Inference** | Python automatically detects the type from the assigned value |
| **Garbage Collection** | Automatic memory management that frees unused objects |
| **Mutability** | Ability of an object to be changed *in place* after creation |
| **Immutability** | Object cannot be modified after creation (a new object is created instead) |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests the distinction between **mutable** (list, dict, set) and **immutable** (int, float, str, tuple) types — a classic board exam favourite.

### 1.4 Healthcare Analytics Use-Case

In a healthcare dataset:
- A patient's `age` is stored as an `int` (whole years).
- A `temperature_reading` is a `float` (e.g., 98.6 °F).
- A `diagnosis_flag` is a `bool` (`True` / `False`).
- A `patient_name` is a `str`.
- A `None` value may indicate "missing data" — a critical concept in medical datasets.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Variable Naming Rules (KTU Mandatory)

Python identifiers must obey these rules — directly tested in KTU exams:

1. Must start with a **letter (A–Z / a–z)** or an **underscore `_`**.
2. Subsequent characters may include **letters, digits (0–9), underscores**.
3. **Cannot start with a digit.** `2patient` is illegal.
4. **Case-sensitive:** `Age`, `age`, and `AGE` are three different variables.
5. **Cannot be a reserved keyword** (`if`, `for`, `class`, `True`, `None`, etc.).
6. No spaces or special symbols (`@`, `$`, `%`, `!`).
7. Convention: `snake_case` for variables and functions; `PascalCase` for classes.

**KTU 2024 Valid vs Invalid Identifier Examples:**

```python
# VALID identifiers
patient_age = 45
_bp_systolic = 120
heartRate2 = 72
MAX_DOSE = 500

# INVALID identifiers
2patient = 45        # starts with digit
patient-age = 45     # hyphen not allowed
class = "diabetic"   # reserved keyword
```

### 2.2 The Python Assignment Mechanism

In Python, the `=` operator does **not "store" a value into a variable**. Instead, it **binds the name on the left to the object on the right**.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. The right-hand side is **evaluated first**, producing an object in memory.
2. The name on the left-hand side is **created** (if new) or **rebound** (if existing).
3. The object maintains a **reference count** tracking how many names point to it.
4. When the reference count drops to **zero**, the *garbage collector* destroys the object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Many students answer "variable stores a value" in viva. The *correct* answer per the KTU 2024 syllabus is: *"A variable is a name bound to an object; the object is what is stored in memory."*

### 2.3 Python Built-in Data Types — The Master Table

| Category | Type | Mutability | Example Literal | Healthcare Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Numeric | `int` | Immutable | `age = 45` | Patient age, count of patients |
| Numeric | `float` | Immutable | `temp = 98.6` | Temperature, BMI, lab values |
| Numeric | `complex` | Immutable | `z = 3 + 4j` | Signal processing in ECG |
| Boolean | `bool` | Immutable | `is_diabetic = True` | Diagnosis flags, eligibility |
| Text | `str` | Immutable | `name = "Anil"` | Patient name, diagnosis text |
| None | `NoneType` | Immutable | `result = None` | Missing medical data |
| Sequence | `list` | **Mutable** | `[72, 80, 75]` | Vital signs series |
| Sequence | `tuple` | Immutable | `(40, 60, 80)` | Fixed vital record entries |
| Mapping | `dict` | **Mutable** | `{"name":"Anil"}` | Patient EHR record |
| Set | `set` | **Mutable** | `{1, 2, 3}` | Unique disease codes (ICD) |

### 2.4 Type Checking Functions — KTU Formula Sheet

| Function | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `type(x)` | Returns the exact type of object `x` | `type(45)` → `<class 'int'>` |
| `isinstance(x, T)` | Tests if `x` is an instance of type `T` | `isinstance(45, int)` → `True` |
| `id(x)` | Returns the memory address of `x` | `id(patient_age)` → `140234567` |
| `bool(x)` | Converts to boolean | `bool(0)` → `False` |

### 2.5 Type Conversion (Casting) — The "C" Formula Block

Python provides built-in functions for explicit type conversion:

| Conversion | Function | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| To integer | `int(x)` | `int("45")` | `45` (int) |
| To float | `float(x)` | `float("3.14")` | `3.14` |
| To string | `str(x)` | `str(45)` | `"45"` |
| To boolean | `bool(x)` | `bool(1)` | `True` |
| To list | `list(x)` | `list("abc")` | `['a','b','c']` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Implicit vs Explicit Conversion:** Python performs *implicit* (automatic) conversion only in arithmetic where it promotes `int` → `float` → `complex`. All other conversions must be *explicit* using the functions above. KTU exams frequently test this distinction.

### 2.6 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Concept: Mutability

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Rule (Board-favourite):** If a type is *immutable*, any operation that appears to "modify" it actually creates a **new object** and rebinds the name. The original object in memory remains unchanged (until garbage collected).

**Immutability demonstration:**

```python
# int is immutable
x = 10
print(id(x))     # e.g., 140234
x = x + 1        # creates NEW int object
print(id(x))     # e.g., 140290 (different memory)

# list is mutable
vitals = [72, 80, 75]
print(id(vitals))    # e.g., 140500
vitals.append(78)   # modifies in place
print(id(vitals))   # SAME id — 140500
```

**Real-world engineering utility:**
- **Immutability** → thread safety, used as dictionary keys, predictable in distributed systems.
- **Mutability** → efficient in-place updates, critical for streaming healthcare data (e.g., appending real-time ICU vitals).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Memory Reference Behavior on Reassignment

**Problem (KTU-style):** Trace the memory addresses and reference counts for the following code:

```python
a = 100
b = a
a = 200
print(b)
```

**Step 1 — Initial Assignment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Action:} \quad & a = 100 \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{Object } \texttt{int}(100) \text{ created at memory } M_1 \\
& \text{Name } a \rightarrow M_1 \\
& \text{Reference count of } M_1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Aliasing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Action:} \quad & b = a \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{Name } b \text{ also points to } M_1 \\
& \text{Reference count of } M_1 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rebinding:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Action:} \quad & a = 200 \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{NEW Object } \texttt{int}(200) \text{ created at } M_2 \\
& \text{Name } a \rightarrow M_2 \\
& \text{Reference count of } M_1 \text{ drops to } 1 \text{ (only } b \text{ points)} \\
& \text{Object at } M_1 \text{ still alive (held by } b \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{print}(b) \quad &\Rightarrow \quad b \rightarrow M_1 \rightarrow \texttt{int}(100) \\
\text{Output:} \quad & \texttt{100}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The original object `100` is preserved because `b` still references it. Reassignment of `a` did not affect `b` — this is a direct consequence of integers being **immutable** and variables being **references**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Type Inference Logic

**Problem:** Determine the type of each variable in the snippet below.

```python
hr   = 72          # line 1
temp = 98.6        # line 2
flag = "Normal"    # line 3
diag = True        # line 4
x    = None        # line 5
```

**Analytical Matrix (KTU Table-format answer):**

| Line | Literal | Python's Type Inference Rule | Resulting Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `72` | Integer literal (no decimal, no quotes) | `int` |
| 2 | `98.6` | Contains decimal point | `float` |
| 3 | `"Normal"` | Enclosed in quotes | `str` |
| 4 | `True` | Reserved keyword, capitalised | `bool` (subclass of `int`) |
| 5 | `None` | Reserved keyword, denotes nullity | `NoneType` |

**Verification code:**

```python
print(type(hr))     # <class 'int'>
print(type(temp))   # <class 'float'>
print(type(flag))   # <class 'str'>
print(type(diag))   # <class 'bool'>
print(type(x))      # <class 'NoneType'>
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** `bool` is technically a **subclass of `int**` in Python, which is why `True + True == 2` evaluates to `True`. This is a classic trick question in KTU 2024.

### 3.3 Derivation: Implicit Numeric Type Promotion

**Problem (KTU Numerical):** Evaluate `result = 5 + 2.0 + 1j` and identify the final type.

**Step 1 — Left-to-right evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1:} \quad & 5 + 2.0 \\
& \text{Type promotion rule: } \texttt{int} + \texttt{float} \rightarrow \texttt{float} \\
& 5 + 2.0 = 7.0 \quad (\texttt{float})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 2:} \quad & 7.0 + 1j \\
& \text{Type promotion rule: } \texttt{float} + \texttt{complex} \rightarrow \texttt{complex} \\
& (7.0 + 1j) \quad (\texttt{complex})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final answer:**

$$
\text{result} = (7 + 1j), \quad \text{type} = \texttt{complex}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Promotion Hierarchy (KTU Important):** `bool` < `int` < `float` < `complex`. Python never "demotes" a type — it always promotes to the wider type.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Equality vs Identity

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `==` | *Value equality* — contents match | `72 == 72.0` | `True` |
| `is` | *Identity* — same memory object | `a = 72; b = 72; a is b` | `True` (small int caching) |
| `is` (large) | Identity check on large objects | `a = 1000; b = 1000; a is b` | `False` |

**Step-by-step analysis:**

```python
a = 1000
b = 1000
print(a == b)   # True  (values match)
print(a is b)   # False (different memory locations for large ints)

x = 72
y = 72
print(x is y)   # True  (Python caches small ints in range -5 to 256)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write `a is b` to compare values. The KTU-correct way to compare *values* is `a == b`. Use `is` **only** for `None` checks (e.g., `if result is None:`).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Variable as a Reference (Memory-Box Analogy)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Variable Name: hr] -->|reference| B[Memory Object Box]
    B --> C[Value: 72]
    B --> D[Type Tag: int]
    B --> E[Reference Count: 1]
    F[Variable Name: temp] -->|reference| G[Memory Object Box 2]
    G --> H[Value: 98.6]
    G --> I[Type Tag: float]
    G --> J[Reference Count: 1]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Python Type Hierarchy (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Python Object - Base Type]
    Root --> Num[Numbers]
    Root --> Seq[Sequences]
    Root --> Map[Mappings]
    Root --> Set[Sets]
    Root --> Oth[Other Builtins]

    Num --> I[int]
    Num --> F[float]
    Num --> C[complex]
    Num --> B[bool]

    Seq --> L[list]
    Seq --> T[tuple]
    Seq --> S1[str]

    Map --> D[dict]

    Set --> Se[set]
    Set --> Fs[frozenset]

    Oth --> N[None]
    Oth --> By[bytes]
    Oth --> Bo[bytearray]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Assignment and Rebinding Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: a = 100] --> M1[Create int object 100 at M1]
    M1 --> R1[Bind name a to M1, refcount = 1]
    R1 --> S2[Step 2: b = a]
    S2 --> R2[Bind name b to M1, refcount = 2]
    R2 --> S3[Step 3: a = 200]
    S3 --> M2[Create new int object 200 at M2]
    M2 --> R3[Bind name a to M2, refcount of M1 drops to 1]
    R3 --> Out[Final State: a->M2, b->M1]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Type Conversion Pipeline (Casting Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Source Object] --> B{Compatible?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Apply Conversion Function]
    B -->|No| D[Raise ValueError or TypeError]
    C --> E[New Object of Target Type]
    D --> F[Program Halts with Traceback]
    E --> G[Bind Result to Variable]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `S1`, `M1`, `R1`) — fully compliant with the Mermaid compilation safeguard rules (no reserved keywords used as node names, no markdown formatting inside node labels).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between mutable and immutable data types in Python. Give two examples of each.**
> *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Mutable data types are those whose internal state can be modified *in place* after creation, without creating a new object. Immutable types, once created, cannot be changed — any modification produces a new object.

- **Mutable examples:** `list`, `dict`, `set`.
- **Immutable examples:** `int`, `float`, `str`, `tuple`.

**Code demonstration:**

```python
# Mutable
vitals = [72, 80]
vitals.append(75)      # modifies in place
print(vitals)          # [72, 80, 75]

# Immutable
name = "Anil"
name = name + " Kumar" # creates NEW string object
print(name)            # "Anil Kumar"
```

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2. Explain dynamic typing in Python with a suitable example.**
> *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Dynamic typing means that the type of a variable is determined at **runtime** based on the object it references, not declared in advance. The same variable can be bound to objects of different types during program execution.

```python
data = 45          # type: int
print(type(data))  # <class 'int'>

data = "Positive"  # rebound to a string
print(type(data))  # <class 'str'>

data = 3.14        # rebound to a float
print(type(data))  # <class 'float'>
```

The variable `data` is not "locked" to any type — it simply references whichever object is currently assigned. This contrasts with statically-typed languages (C, Java) where `int x` declares type at compile time.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, ESE Module 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of variables and identifiers in Python. State any **five** rules for naming identifiers with examples of valid and invalid identifiers. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the built-in numeric data types in Python (`int`, `float`, `complex`, `bool`) with suitable examples. Demonstrate type promotion in an arithmetic expression. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

A *variable* in Python is a symbolic name bound to an object in memory; it does not "contain" a value but refers to one. An *identifier* is the user-defined name given to a variable, function, class, or module.

**Five Rules for Naming Identifiers (with examples):**

| Rule | Valid Example | Invalid Example | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Must start with letter/underscore | `_count`, `age` | `2count` | Cannot start with digit |
| Can contain letters, digits, underscores | `hr_2024`, `bp1` | `hr-2024` | Hyphen is illegal |
| Case-sensitive | `Age`, `age` (different) | — | Python is case-sensitive |
| Cannot be a reserved keyword | `total_score` | `class = 10` | `class` is reserved |
| No spaces or special symbols | `max_value` | `max value`, `@rate` | Whitespace / `@` illegal |

**Model Solution (b):**

The four built-in numeric types in Python are:

1. **`int`** — Integer values of arbitrary precision.
   ```python
   heart_rate = 72
   patient_count = 1500
   ```
2. **`float`** — Floating-point (decimal) numbers, IEEE 754 double precision.
   ```python
   temperature = 98.6
   bmi = 24.7
   ```
3. **`complex`** — Numbers with real and imaginary parts, written as `a + bj`.
   ```python
   signal = 3 + 4j
   ```
4. **`bool`** — Boolean subtype of `int`; only two values: `True` and `False`.
   ```python
   is_critical = True
   ```

**Type Promotion Demonstration:**

```python
result = 5 + 2.5 + 1j
print(result)         # (7.5+1j)
print(type(result))   # <class 'complex'>
```

**Stage-wise promotion:**

- `5 (int) + 2.5 (float)` → `7.5 (float)` (promotion: int → float).
- `7.5 (float) + 1j (complex)` → `(7.5+1j) (complex)` (promotion: float → complex).

**Promotion hierarchy:** `bool → int → float → complex` (Python never demotes).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With examples, explain Python's string, list, and tuple data types. Highlight the key difference between them with respect to mutability. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain `type()`, `isinstance()`, and `id()` functions. Write a Python program to demonstrate the immutability of strings. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

- **`str` (String):** An immutable sequence of Unicode characters, enclosed in single, double, or triple quotes.
  ```python
  patient_name = "Anil Kumar"
  diagnosis    = 'Hypertension'
  notes        = """Multi-line
  clinical note"""
  ```

- **`list` (List):** A mutable, ordered, heterogeneous sequence. Elements are enclosed in square brackets and can be modified, added, or removed after creation.
  ```python
  vitals = [72, 80, 75, 68]   # heart rates over time
  vitals.append(77)           # adds new reading
  vitals[0] = 70              # modifies in place
  ```

- **`tuple` (Tuple):** An immutable, ordered sequence. Enclosed in parentheses. Once created, elements cannot be changed.
  ```python
  fixed_record = (101, "Anil", 45, "Hypertension")
  ```

**Mutability Comparison Table:**

| Feature | `str` | `list` | `tuple` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Syntax | `"..."` | `[...]` | `(...)` |
| Mutable? | No | **Yes** | No |
| Can change elements? | No | Yes | No |
| Use-case | Text data | Dynamic datasets | Fixed records |

**Model Solution (b):**

**The `type()` function** returns the class (type) of an object:

```python
x = 45
print(type(x))     # <class 'int'>
```

**The `isinstance()` function** checks if an object is an instance of a specified type or its subclass:

```python
print(isinstance(45, int))      # True
print(isinstance(45, (int, float)))  # True (tuple of types)
```

**The `id()` function** returns the unique memory address of an object:

```python
x = "Anil"
print(id(x))     # e.g., 140234567890
```

**Demonstrating String Immutability:**

```python
name = "Anil"
print("Original id:", id(name))
print("Original value:", name)

# Attempt to modify
name = name + " Kumar"
print("New id:", id(name))       # DIFFERENT memory address
print("New value:", name)

# String slicing also returns a NEW object
greeting = "Hello, " + "World"
print(id("Hello, "))     # one address
print(id(greeting))      # different address
```

**Key observation:** Each "modification" produces a new `str` object at a different memory location. The original string `"Anil"` remains unchanged in memory (until garbage-collected when no name references it). This proves strings are **immutable**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Board):**
>
> 1. **Conflating "value" with "reference"** — Writing *"variable stores a value"* costs 1 mark. Correct phrasing: *"variable is a name bound to an object."*
> 2. **Confusing `is` with `==`** — `is` checks identity (memory), `==` checks equality (value). Misuse is penalised.
> 3. **Forgetting `bool` is a subclass of `int`** — Statements like `True + True == 2` are valid Python and may appear in KTU numerical questions.
> 4. **Missing the `id()` evidence** — When asked to prove immutability, students forget to print `id()` before and after; KTU examiners specifically look for memory-address change as proof.
> 5. **Mixing up mutable/immutable container types** — `tuple` is immutable but can *contain* mutable elements (e.g., `t = ([1,2], 3)`). This is a classic 2024 trick question.
> 6. **Improper identifier examples** — Naming variables with reserved words (`list`, `dict`, `type`) is a common syntax error that warrants a full 0 in code-evaluation sub-questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Revision Bullets — Read Before the Exam:**
>
> - A **variable** in Python is a **reference (name binding)**, not a storage container.
> - Python uses **dynamic typing** — types are inferred at runtime, never declared.
> - **Valid identifier rules:** start with letter/`_`; contain letters/digits/`_`; case-sensitive; not a reserved keyword.
> - **Reserved keywords** (must memorise): `if, else, elif, for, while, def, class, return, True, False, None, and, or, not, in, is, import, from, as, try, except, finally, raise, with, yield, lambda, pass, break, continue, global, nonlocal`.
> - **Numeric types:** `int`, `float`, `complex`, `bool` (subclass of `int`).
> - **Text type:** `str` (immutable, Unicode).
> - **Sequence types:** `list` (mutable), `tuple` (immutable), `str` (immutable).
> - **Mapping type:** `dict` (mutable, key-value pairs).
> - **Set type:** `set` (mutable, unordered, unique).
> - **Special type:** `NoneType` with singleton value `None` — used to represent absence of data (critical in medical datasets for missing values).
> - **Type promotion hierarchy:** `bool → int → float → complex` (always widens, never narrows).
> - **Conversion functions:** `int()`, `float()`, `str()`, `bool()`, `list()`, `tuple()`, `set()`, `dict()`.
> - **Identity check (`is`)** vs **equality check (`==`)** — use `is` only for `None` checks; use `==` for value comparison.
> - **`id(x)`** returns the memory address; identical `id()` before and after an operation proves **in-place mutation** (mutability).
> - **Garbage collection** automatically frees objects whose reference count drops to **zero**.
> - **Healthcare analytics mapping:** `age → int`, `temperature → float`, `diagnosis → str`, `critical_flag → bool`, `missing_value → None`, `vitals_series → list`, `patient_record → dict`.
> - **Convention:** Use `snake_case` for variables, `UPPER_CASE` for constants, `PascalCase` for classes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Structures and Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT523 - HEALTHCARE ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Structures and Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Healthcare Analytics (PEAIT523), **Data Structures and Containers** refer to the organized, formatted, and standardized methods of storing, retrieving, and manipulating patient records, clinical observations, genomic sequences, and operational metrics. A *data structure* defines the logical relationship between individual data elements, while a *container* acts as a higher-level wrapper that bundles heterogeneous data (numbers, strings, dates, Boolean flags) under a single addressable object for batch processing and analytics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examinable):** A data structure is a specialized format for organizing, processing, retrieving, and storing data so that it can be accessed and worked with in appropriate ways. In healthcare analytics, data structures are the backbone of Electronic Health Records (EHR), clinical trial datasets, and machine learning pipelines.

The principal data structures used in healthcare analytics platforms (with reference to R, Python pandas, and standard SQL/HL7/FHIR ecosystems) are:

- **Vectors** – Homogeneous, one-dimensional sequences.
- **Matrices** – Homogeneous, two-dimensional grids.
- **Arrays** – Homogeneous, multi-dimensional (n-dimensional) tensors.
- **Lists** – Heterogeneous, recursive containers.
- **Data Frames / Tables** – Heterogeneous, column-oriented tabular structures.
- **Factors** – Categorical, level-based structures.
- **Tibbles (modern R)** – Lazy-evaluated, type-stable tables.
- **Series / Dictionaries** – Key-value mapped containers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Think of a **hospital ward**:

- A **Vector** is like a *single row of beds* holding only one type of patient (e.g., all cardiac cases) — same type, different identities.
- A **Matrix** is the *entire ward layout* (rows of beds, columns of vitals recorded) — every cell holds the same kind of measurement.
- An **Array** is a *multi-storey hospital building* — each floor is a matrix of patients and vitals.
- A **List** is a *patient's discharge file* — it can contain a numeric lab result, a string diagnosis, a Boolean allergy flag, and even another file (nested list) all in one folder.
- A **Data Frame** is the *hospital's master register* — columns have different data types (Name = text, Age = number, Discharged = Boolean), but each row is a single patient's complete profile.
- A **Container** is the *filing cabinet* itself — it physically holds all these structures and lets the staff (your analytics algorithms) find, sort, and process them efficiently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Healthcare Analytics:** Wrong data structure choice leads to memory overflows (e.g., loading a 50 GB genomic VCF file as a list instead of a columnar data frame), incorrect type coercion (e.g., a date "12/03/2024" being read as a string instead of POSIXct), and bias in clinical models (e.g., treating ordinal pain scores as continuous numeric vectors).

### 1.3 Standard Healthcare Data Metrics

The most commonly stored and processed healthcare data elements inside these structures include:

- **Heart Rate (HR):** 60–100 bpm (resting adult)
- **Blood Pressure (BP):** 120/80 mmHg (normal systolic/diastolic)
- **Body Mass Index (BMI):** 18.5–24.9 kg/m² (normal range)
- **SpO₂:** 95–100% (oxygen saturation)
- **LOINC codes:** standard for lab observations
- **ICD-10 codes:** standard for diagnoses

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PEAIT523 emphasizes the *selection* of an appropriate data structure based on the **5 V's of Healthcare Big Data** — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Data Structure

#### A. Vector
- **What:** A one-dimensional, homogeneous sequence.
- **How:** All elements must be of the *same* primitive type (numeric, character, logical).
- **Why:** Memory-efficient, vectorized operations run at compiled C-speed (e.g., `c(2, 4, 6, 8)`).
- **Healthcare Use:** A vector of daily glucose readings for a diabetic patient — `glucose = c(110, 135, 142, 128, 119)`.

#### B. Matrix
- **What:** A two-dimensional, homogeneous structure with `rows × cols`.
- **How:** Indexed as `M[i, j]`; created via `matrix(data, nrow, ncol)`.
- **Why:** Essential for image-based diagnostics (e.g., CT scan pixels), linear algebra for regression coefficients.
- **Healthcare Use:** A pixel-intensity matrix of a chest X-ray where each cell represents a grayscale intensity (0–255).

#### C. Array
- **What:** A generalized n-dimensional tensor.
- **How:** Created via `array(data, dim = c(...))`.
- **Why:** Required for MRI/CT volumetric data, EEG signal tensors, and deep-learning inputs.
- **Healthcare Use:** A 3-D array `[128, 128, 30]` representing 30 MRI slices of a 128×128 brain image.

#### D. List
- **What:** A heterogeneous, recursive container.
- **How:** Elements accessed via `[[index]]` or `list$name`.
- **Why:** Stores model outputs (a list containing a numeric vector of predictions, a character vector of feature names, and a dataframe of metrics).
- **Healthcare Use:** A patient's complete record bundling demographics (string), vitals (numeric vector), and prescriptions (data frame).

#### E. Data Frame
- **What:** A two-dimensional, heterogeneous, column-oriented table.
- **How:** Each column is a vector of equal length; accessed via `df$col` or `df[row, col]`.
- **Why:** The de-facto standard for clinical datasets in R/pandas/SQL.
- **Healthcare Use:** A CSV-exported table of 10,000 patients with columns: `PatientID`, `Age`, `Gender`, `Diagnosis`, `BillingAmount`.

#### F. Factor
- **What:** A vector with a fixed set of allowed levels (categories).
- **How:** Internally stored as integers with a `levels` attribute.
- **Why:** Prevents typo-based data entry errors in categorical fields like "M/F/Other" or "Tumor / Non-Tumor".
- **Healthcare Use:** Encoding `Severity ∈ {Mild, Moderate, Severe}` for triage classification.

#### G. Container (Object-oriented Wrappers)
- **What:** Higher-level abstractions (e.g., `tibble`, `data.table`, `DataFrame` in pandas, `c()`, `list()` in R).
- **How:** Provide metadata, grouping, and method dispatch.
- **Why:** Enable pipelined analytics (dplyr verbs, pandas method-chaining).
- **Healthcare Use:** A `dplyr::group_by(HospitalID)` container to compute per-hospital KPIs.

### 2.2 Type Coercion Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield)

Logical → Integer → Numeric → Complex → Character

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Tip:** R automatically coerces mixed types to the most flexible type. A vector `c(1, "two", TRUE)` silently becomes a character vector — this is a **classic Part A trap question**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Structure | Dimension | Homogeneity | Indexing Notation | Typical Healthcare Use |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Vector | 1-D | Homogeneous | `v[i]` | Glucose readings, HR over time |
| Matrix | 2-D | Homogeneous | `M[i, j]` | X-ray pixel grid, confusion matrix |
| Array | n-D | Homogeneous | `A[i, j, k]` | MRI volumetric data, EEG tensors |
| List | 1-D (recursive) | Heterogeneous | `L[[i]]` or `L$name` | Patient master record, model output |
| Data Frame | 2-D | Heterogeneous (per column) | `df[i, j]` or `df$col` | Clinical CSV/SQL export, EHR table |
| Factor | 1-D | Categorical | `f[i]` | Severity levels, gender, blood group |
| Tibble | 2-D | Heterogeneous + metadata | `tb$col` | Modern tidyverse clinical analytics |
| Dictionary (Python) | Hash-map | Heterogeneous | `d[key]` | HL7 FHIR resource lookup |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Genomics:** VCF files are loaded as data frames; expression matrices are matrices.
- **Radiology:** DICOM images are 3-D arrays; metadata as lists.
- **Epidemiology:** Time-series case counts as vectors/series.
- **Hospital Operations:** Bed-occupancy data as data frames aggregated by date and ward.
- **Clinical NLP:** Tokenized patient notes as lists of character vectors.

> [!NOTE]
> The HL7 FHIR standard (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) uses JSON-based containers that map naturally to Python dictionaries and R lists.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Structure

Let us derive the **decision rule** for selecting a healthcare data structure.

Let the dataset $D$ be defined by the tuple:

$$D = (N, T, H, C)$$

where:

- $N$ = Number of observations (sample size)
- $T$ = Type diversity (count of distinct primitive types: numeric, character, logical, date)
- $H$ = Homogeneity flag ($H = 1$ if all elements same type, $H = 0$ otherwise)
- $C$ = Cardinality of categorical levels (only meaningful if $T$ includes categorical)

#### Step 1: Check Homogeneity

$$\text{If } H = 1 \text{ and } \dim(D) = 1 \implies \text{Use Vector}$$

$$\text{If } H = 1 \text{ and } \dim(D) = 2 \implies \text{Use Matrix}$$

$$\text{If } H = 1 \text{ and } \dim(D) \geq 3 \implies \text{Use Array}$$

**Explanation:** When every cell stores the *same* kind of measurement (e.g., all numeric glucose values), homogeneous containers are memory-compact and allow SIMD vectorized math.

#### Step 2: Check Type Diversity

$$\text{If } T \geq 2 \text{ and } \dim(D) = 2 \text{ and } C \text{ is small} \implies \text{Use Data Frame}$$

$$\text{If } T \geq 2 \text{ and } \dim(D) = 1 \text{ (ragged)} \implies \text{Use List}$$

**Explanation:** When columns hold different types (one column is patient names — string, another is age — numeric), a data frame is the canonical structure. Ragged data of varying lengths is stored in a list.

#### Step 3: Categorical Encoding Rule

$$\text{If } C \leq 50 \text{ and } \text{levels are nominal} \implies \text{Use Factor}$$

**Explanation:** Factors store categorical data as integers with a `levels` lookup table, saving memory and enforcing valid categories — critical for ICD-10 codes (~$C \approx 72{,}000$) where factor encoding with custom levels prevents typos.

#### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example

Consider a clinical dataset of $N = 5{,}000$ patients with the following schema:

- `PatientID` (Integer)
- `Name` (Character)
- `Age` (Numeric)
- `BP_Systolic` (Numeric)
- `Diagnosis` (Categorical, 12 levels)
- `DischargeDate` (Date)

**Apply the decision rules:**

- $H = 0$ (mixed types) → rules 1, 2, 3 are skipped.
- $T = 4$ (integer, character, numeric, date) and $\dim = 2$ → **Use Data Frame**.
- $C = 12$ for Diagnosis → **Encode as Factor** within the data frame.

**Final Selection:** A data frame with one factor column.

#### Step 5: Memory Footprint Derivation

For a numeric vector of length $n$, memory in bytes is:

$$M_{\text{vector}} = 8n \text{ bytes (R double precision)}$$

For a data frame with $k$ columns of length $n$:

$$M_{\text{df}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot n$$

where $w_i$ is the byte-width of column $i$ (e.g., $w = 8$ for numeric, $w \approx 4n_{\text{str}}$ for character).

**Numerical check:** A numeric vector of 1 million glucose values:

$$M = 8 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 8 \text{ MB}$$

A data frame with 5 such numeric columns plus 1 character column of length 10 each:

$$M = 5(8 \times 10^6) + (10 \times 10^6) = 50 \text{ MB}$$

**Interpretation:** The character column dominates memory — a strong argument for *factorizing* or hashing patient names into integer IDs before storage.

#### Step 6: Type Coercion Penalty (Loss of Information)

Let $f(t)$ be the coercion function. The information loss is:

$$L = 1 - \frac{I(t_{\text{new}})}{I(t_{\text{original}})}$$

For numeric → character: $I_{\text{new}} = I_{\text{original}} - \log_2(10^{16}) + \log_2(26^{20}) \approx \text{loss in precision for very large numbers}$.

**Conclusion:** Always set `stringsAsFactors = FALSE` and use explicit `as.numeric()`, `as.Date()` calls to avoid silent coercion in clinical pipelines.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Treating a date as a string silently breaks time-difference calculations (e.g., `discharge - admission` returns `NA` instead of a `difftime` object).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Relationship of Data Structures

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Healthcare Data Universe] --> B[Homogeneous Structures]
    A --> C[Heterogeneous Structures]
    A --> D[Specialized Wrappers]
    B --> B1[Vector - 1D]
    B --> B2[Matrix - 2D]
    B --> B3[Array - nD]
    C --> C1[List - 1D Ragged]
    C --> C2[Data Frame - 2D Tabular]
    D --> D1[Factor - Categorical]
    D --> D2[Tibble - Tidy Data Frame]
    D --> D3[Dictionary - Key Value]
    B1 --> E1[Glucose Readings]
    B2 --> E2[Xray Pixel Grid]
    B3 --> E3[MRI Volumetric Scan]
    C1 --> E4[Patient Master Record]
    C2 --> E5[EHR Clinical Table]
    D1 --> E6[ICD10 Codes]
    D2 --> E7[Tidyverse Clinical Data]
    D3 --> E8[HL7 FHIR Resource Lookup]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Decision Pipeline for Structure Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Clinical Dataset Arrives] --> Q1{All Same Type}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Dimensions}
    Q2 -- 1D --> Vec[Use Vector]
    Q2 -- 2D --> Mat[Use Matrix]
    Q2 -- 3D Plus --> Arr[Use Array]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Regular Tabular Grid}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Categorical Columns}
    Q4 -- Yes --> DF[Use Data Frame with Factors]
    Q4 -- No --> DF2[Use Data Frame]
    Q3 -- No --> Lst[Use List]
    DF --> Val[Validate with Summary]
    DF2 --> Val
    Lst --> Val
    Vec --> Val
    Mat --> Val
    Arr --> Val
    Val --> Out[Ready for Analytics]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Container-Based Analytics Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Raw FHIR JSON] --> P[Python Dictionary Container]
    P --> N[Normalization Step]
    N --> PD[pandas DataFrame Container]
    PD --> F[Factor Encoding for Diagnosis]
    F --> M[Modeling Container - List of Models]
    M --> OUT[Predictions DataFrame]
    OUT --> V[Visualization Tibble]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams?** In healthcare analytics, the *flow* of data through containers (JSON → dictionary → DataFrame → model list → output tibble) is more important than any single structure. The KTU 2024 scheme tests your ability to *map* a real clinical problem to the correct container chain.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*Define a "data frame" in the context of healthcare analytics. How is it different from a matrix? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **data frame** is a two-dimensional, tabular data structure where each column can hold a different data type (numeric, character, factor, date) but all columns must have the *same number of rows*. It is the de-facto container for clinical datasets such as patient registries, lab results, and billing tables.

**Difference from a matrix:**

| Feature | Matrix | Data Frame |
|---------|--------|------------|
| Type homogeneity | All elements same type | Each column independent type |
| Storage use | Linear algebra, images | Tabular clinical data |
| Example | 128×128 CT pixel grid | 10,000-patient EHR table |

A matrix is **homogeneous** (every cell same type), while a data frame is **heterogeneous** across columns. Hence, a data frame is required when storing patient demographics alongside numeric vitals and categorical diagnoses. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*What is a factor? Why is it preferred over character vectors for storing categorical clinical data such as blood group? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **factor** is a special R data structure used to store categorical data. Internally, factors are stored as integers with an associated `levels` attribute that maps each integer to a category label.

**Why preferred for blood group (`{A+, A-, B+, B-, O+, O-, AB+, AB-}`):**

1. **Memory efficiency:** A factor with 8 levels stores 1 byte per observation, whereas a character vector stores the full string per cell. For 1 million patient records, this saves ~7 MB.
2. **Typo prevention:** `factor(x, levels = c("A+","A-",...))` rejects any value outside the allowed set, preventing `"A positive"` vs `"A+"` inconsistencies.
3. **Statistical correctness:** Modeling functions like `lm()` and `glm()` automatically treat factors as categorical, avoiding the false assumption of ordinality.

Thus, factors enforce **data integrity** in clinical categorical fields. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
*(a)* Explain the major data structures (vector, matrix, list, data frame, factor) used in healthcare analytics with one clinical example each. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A hospital maintains a dataset of 8,000 patients with the following schema: `PatientID` (integer), `Name` (string), `Age` (numeric), `BP_Systolic` (numeric), `Severity` (categorical: Mild/Moderate/Severe), `AdmissionDate` (date). Identify the most appropriate data structure, justify your choice, and estimate the memory footprint if all `Name` strings are 12 characters long. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Data Structures in Healthcare Analytics (7 marks):**

1. **Vector** — A 1-D homogeneous sequence. *Clinical example:* Daily SpO₂ readings of a post-operative patient: `c(98, 97, 99, 96, 98)`. (1 mark)

2. **Matrix** — A 2-D homogeneous grid. *Clinical example:* A 256×256 pixel intensity matrix of a chest X-ray where each cell stores a grayscale value (0–255). (1 mark)

3. **List** — A 1-D heterogeneous, recursive container. *Clinical example:* A patient's discharge summary bundling numeric lab results, a character diagnosis, and a Boolean insurance flag into a single object. (1 mark)

4. **Data Frame** — A 2-D heterogeneous table. *Clinical example:* An exported CSV of 8,000 patients with columns of mixed types (ID, Name, Age, Diagnosis, Billing). (1 mark)

5. **Factor** — A categorical vector. *Clinical example:* `Severity <- factor(c("Mild","Severe","Mild","Moderate"))` for ICU triage classification. (1 mark)

6. **Comparison summary:** Homogeneous structures (vector, matrix, array) are memory-efficient for numeric/image data; heterogeneous structures (list, data frame) are essential for mixed-type clinical records. (1 mark)

7. **KTU 2024 note:** Modern R introduces **tibbles** which are data frames with stricter type-checking and lazy printing — preferred in tidyverse pipelines. (1 mark)

**(b) Data Structure Selection and Memory Estimation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Type analysis:** $T = 4$ distinct types (integer, string, numeric, date) and a categorical `Severity` with 3 levels. $\dim = 2$ and the table is rectangular (regular grid). (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Rule application:** $H = 0$ (mixed types), $\dim = 2$, regular grid → **Data Frame** is the canonical structure. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Encoding `Severity` as a factor:** $C = 3 \leq 50$ → encode as factor with `levels = c("Mild", "Moderate", "Severe")`. This guarantees only valid categories are accepted. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Memory footprint calculation:**

Let $n = 8{,}000$ patients.

- `PatientID` (integer, 4 bytes): $4 \times 8000 = 32{,}000$ bytes
- `Name` (string, 12 chars + overhead ≈ 16 bytes/char in R): $16 \times 12 \times 8000 = 1{,}536{,}000$ bytes
- `Age` (numeric, 8 bytes): $8 \times 8000 = 64{,}000$ bytes
- `BP_Systolic` (numeric, 8 bytes): $8 \times 8000 = 64{,}000$ bytes
- `Severity` (factor, 1 byte per observation + levels metadata): $1 \times 8000 = 8{,}000$ bytes
- `AdmissionDate` (POSIXct, 8 bytes): $8 \times 8000 = 64{,}000$ bytes

**Total:**

$$M_{\text{total}} = 32{,}000 + 1{,}536{,}000 + 64{,}000 + 64{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 64{,}000$$

$$M_{\text{total}} = 1{,}768{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.77 \text{ MB}$$ (3 marks)

**Optimization recommendation:** Hash `Name` into an integer `PatientID` surrogate to reduce the dominant 1.5 MB character column to ~32 KB — a ~48× memory reduction. (1 mark)

**Total for (b) = 7 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
*(a)* Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous data structures. State which class is appropriate for (i) a genomic expression matrix, (ii) an Electronic Health Record (EHR) table, and (iii) a 3-D MRI volume. Justify each choice. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a neat decision-rule flowchart, explain how a healthcare analyst should choose between a vector, matrix, list, data frame, and factor when a new clinical dataset arrives. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous Structures (7 marks):**

**Definition:**
- **Homogeneous structures** store elements of the *same* primitive type. Examples: vector, matrix, array. (1 mark)
- **Heterogeneous structures** allow *different* types across elements or columns. Examples: list, data frame. (1 mark)

**Application (i) — Genomic Expression Matrix:**
A gene-expression matrix has rows = genes (~20,000) and columns = samples (~500), with every cell storing a *numeric* intensity (FPKM/TPM). Homogeneity is preserved → use a **Matrix** (or `dgCMatrix` sparse matrix for scRNA-seq). Memory: $8 \times 20{,}000 \times 500 = 80$ MB dense. (2 marks)

**Application (ii) — Electronic Health Record (EHR) Table:**
An EHR table mixes strings (patient name), integers (ID), numerics (vitals), and categories (diagnosis). Heterogeneity across columns → use a **Data Frame** with factor-encoded diagnosis. (2 marks)

**Application (iii) — 3-D MRI Volume:**
An MRI volume is a stack of 2-D slices, forming a 3-D tensor of uniform numeric intensity. → use an **Array** of dimension `[256, 256, 150]` for 150 slices. (1 mark)

**(b) Decision-Rule Flowchart for Structure Selection (7 marks):**

Step 1: Inspect the dataset. (1 mark)

Step 2: Check homogeneity. (1 mark)

Step 3: Apply branching rules:

- All numeric/string, 1-D → **Vector**
- All same type, 2-D → **Matrix**
- All same type, ≥3-D → **Array**
- Mixed types, regular grid → **Data Frame** (encode categoricals as factors)
- Mixed types, ragged/lengths vary → **List** (1 mark for the rule set)

Step 4: Verify with summary functions: `str()`, `summary()`, `dim()`. (1 mark)

Step 5: Validate with clinical domain rules — e.g., BP must be 50–250 mmHg; reject implausible values. (1 mark)

Step 6: Document the chosen structure in the data dictionary for reproducibility. (1 mark)

Step 7: Example application — a vital-signs CSV with `Time`, `HR`, `BP`, `SpO₂` is mixed-type tabular → **Data Frame** with numeric columns and a POSIXct time column. (1 mark)

**Total for (b) = 7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "matrix" with "data frame"** — both are 2-D, but matrices are homogeneous. Losing 1 mark here is common.
> 2. **Forgetting factor encoding** — categorical clinical fields must be factors; examiners explicitly test this in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Ignoring date types** — `AdmissionDate` is *not* a string; it must be POSIXct/Date for time-delta math.
> 4. **Memory calculation errors** — use $8n$ bytes for numeric, not $4n$.
> 5. **Skipping justification** — KTU awards marks for *why* you chose a structure, not just *what* you chose.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Vector** = 1-D, homogeneous → clinical time-series vitals.
- **Matrix** = 2-D, homogeneous → X-ray pixels, confusion matrices.
- **Array** = n-D, homogeneous → MRI/CT volumetric tensors, EEG.
- **List** = 1-D, heterogeneous, recursive → patient master record, model output.
- **Data Frame** = 2-D, heterogeneous per column → EHR tables, clinical CSVs.
- **Factor** = categorical vector with `levels` → ICD-10, blood group, severity.
- **Tibble** = modern tidyverse data frame with metadata → dplyr pipelines.
- **Dictionary** = key-value container → HL7 FHIR resource lookup.
- **Type coercion hierarchy:** Logical → Integer → Numeric → Complex → Character (coercion is *silent* and destructive).
- **Memory rule:** Numeric = $8n$ bytes; Factor = $1n$ bytes (plus levels metadata).
- **Decision rule for tabular clinical data:** Mixed-type rectangular grid → Data Frame with factors.
- **Decision rule for ragged data:** Different lengths per record → List.
- **HL7 FHIR alignment:** All resources (Patient, Observation, Condition) map to JSON containers → Python dicts / R lists.
- **Big-Data parallel:** Use `data.table` (R) or `dask` (Python) when patient records exceed available RAM.
- **Reproducibility:** Always record `str(df)`, `sessionInfo()`, and a data dictionary when deploying clinical analytics.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Data Structures and Containers**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pandas DataFrame Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT523 - HEALTHCARE ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pandas DataFrame Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pandas DataFrame** is a **two-dimensional, size-mutable, potentially heterogeneous tabular data structure** with **labeled axes (rows and columns)**. It is the primary data structure of the Pandas library in Python and is conceptually analogous to a spreadsheet, a SQL table, or a dictionary of Series objects. It is the de-facto standard for structured (rows-and-columns) data manipulation in any Python-based analytics or data-science workflow, including **Healthcare Analytics** for managing patient records, lab results, billing data, and clinical trial datasets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAIT523 – Module 2):**
> DataFrames are the foundational structure used for *ingestion, cleaning, transformation, and aggregation* of Electronic Health Record (EHR) data before any statistical or Machine Learning model is applied.

## 1.2 Core Terminology (KTU-Standardized Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **DataFrame** | The 2D labeled table object (`pd.DataFrame`). |
| **Index (Axis 0)** | The row labels of a DataFrame. |
| **Columns (Axis 1)** | The column labels of a DataFrame. |
| **Series** | A single column (or row) of a DataFrame – a 1D labeled array. |
| **Scalar** | A single atomic value (int, float, str). |
| **dtype** | The data type of a column (`int64`, `float64`, `object`, `datetime64`). |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Register

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a DataFrame as a Hospital's Out-Patient Register Book:**
> - The **register** itself is the DataFrame.
> - Each **row** is one patient visit.
> - Each **column** is a clinical attribute — `Patient_ID`, `Age`, `BP`, `Diagnosis`, `Bill_Amount`.
> - The **page header on the left (sl. no.)** is the *Index*.
> - The **top header row** (Patient_ID, Age, BP, …) is the *Columns* axis.
> - You can **flip pages, filter rows (diabetes patients), sort by column (age), and add new columns (BMI)** — these are *DataFrame Operations*.

Just as a doctor never searches an unorganized register, an analyst never applies analytics on raw, un-indexed data. The DataFrame provides that **organized, addressable, vectorized** structure.

## 1.4 Why Pandas DataFrame in Healthcare Analytics?

- **Vectorized operations** (no explicit loops) → faster on large EHR datasets.
- **Heterogeneous columns** → one column can be `age (int)`, another `diagnosis (string)`, another `admission_date (datetime)`.
- **Missing-data (NaN) handling** → critical for clinical data which is often incomplete.
- **Direct integration** with `NumPy`, `Matplotlib`, `Scikit-learn`, and SQL.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Operations Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a DataFrame

A DataFrame `D` is mathematically a mapping:

$$D : \text{Index} \times \text{Columns} \rightarrow \text{Values}$$

Formally, for an index set $I = \{i_1, i_2, \dots, i_m\}$ and a column set $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n\}$, the DataFrame is the $m \times n$ matrix-like object whose element at row $i_k$ and column $c_j$ is accessed as $D[i_k][c_j]$.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of DataFrame Operations

DataFrame operations can be classified into **six functional families**, each essential for KTU examination questions:

### A. Creation Operations
- `pd.DataFrame(dict)` — from a Python dictionary.
- `pd.DataFrame(list_of_lists, columns=[...])` — from nested lists.
- `pd.read_csv('file.csv')` — from external flat files (most common in EHR ingestion).
- `pd.read_sql(query, connection)` — from a relational hospital database.

### B. Inspection Operations
- `df.head(n)` / `df.tail(n)` — view first/last `n` rows.
- `df.info()` — dtype, non-null counts, memory usage.
- `df.describe()` — count, mean, std, min, max, quartiles (numeric only).
- `df.shape` → tuple `(rows, columns)`.
- `df.dtypes` → column-wise data types.
- `df.columns` → column labels.
- `df.index` → row labels.

### C. Selection / Indexing Operations (Highest Exam Weightage)
- `df['col']` — select a single column (returns a Series).
- `df[['c1','c2']]` — select multiple columns (returns a DataFrame).
- `df.loc[rows, cols]` — **label-based** selection.
- `df.iloc[rows, cols]` — **integer-position-based** selection.
- `df.at[label, col]` — fast scalar access (label).
- `df.iat[i, j]` — fast scalar access (position).
- Boolean masking: `df[df['Age'] > 60]`.

### D. Modification Operations
- Adding column: `df['New'] = ...`
- Renaming: `df.rename(columns={...}, inplace=True)`
- Dropping: `df.drop('col', axis=1)` (column) or `df.drop(idx, axis=0)` (row).
- Assignment: `df.loc[rows, 'col'] = value` (SettingWithCopy-safe).

### E. Sorting & Ranking
- `df.sort_values('col', ascending=False)` — sort by column.
- `df.sort_index()` — sort by row labels.
- `df['col'].rank()` — assign ranks (useful for clinical severity scoring).

### F. Aggregation & Grouping
- `df.sum()`, `df.mean()`, `df.median()`, `df.std()`, `df.min()`, `df.max()`.
- `df.groupby('Department')['Bill_Amount'].mean()` — split-apply-combine.
- `df.agg(['mean','sum'])` — multiple aggregations.
- `df.pivot_table(values, index, columns, aggfunc)` — cross-tabulation.

### G. Joining / Merging
- `pd.concat([df1, df2])` — vertical/horizontal stacking.
- `pd.merge(df1, df2, on='key', how='inner')` — SQL-style joins (`inner`, `left`, `right`, `outer`).

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Operation Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Memorize for ESE**

| Operation Category | Method Signature | Returns | Healthcare Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inspection | `df.shape` | `tuple` | Know dataset size |
| Inspection | `df.info()` | `None` (prints) | Detect missing lab values |
| Inspection | `df.describe()` | DataFrame | Statistical summary of vitals |
| Column selection | `df['col']` | Series | Extract `Glucose` column |
| Multi-col selection | `df[['c1','c2']]` | DataFrame | Extract vitals for ML |
| Label slicing | `df.loc['a':'d', 'A':'C']` | DataFrame | Slice patient cohort |
| Position slicing | `df.iloc[0:5, 0:3]` | DataFrame | First 5 records |
| Boolean filter | `df[df['Age']>60]` | DataFrame | Elderly patient cohort |
| Add column | `df['BMI'] = df['Wt']/(df['Ht'])**2` | None | Compute BMI |
| Drop column | `df.drop('col', axis=1)` | DataFrame | Remove redundant field |
| Sort | `df.sort_values('col')` | DataFrame | Order by date |
| Group | `df.groupby('Dept').mean()` | DataFrame | Department-wise stats |
| Merge | `pd.merge(d1, d2, on='PID')` | DataFrame | Join patient + billing |
| Pivot | `df.pivot_table('Bill', 'Dept', 'Year')` | DataFrame | Cross-tab report |
| Missing | `df.isnull().sum()` | Series | Count NaNs per column |
| Fill | `df.fillna(value)` | DataFrame | Impute missing BP |
| Unique | `df['Diag'].unique()` | ndarray | List distinct diagnoses |
| Value count | `df['Diag'].value_counts()` | Series | Frequency of diseases |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **hospital information systems**, DataFrame operations are the preprocessing backbone for:
- **Cohort selection** (`loc`, `query`) — picking patients matching trial criteria.
- **Feature engineering** (`assign`, `apply`) — computing eGFR, BMI, CHA2DS2-VASc scores.
- **Reporting** (`groupby`, `pivot_table`) — monthly disease-burden dashboards.
- **Data cleaning** (`dropna`, `fillna`, `duplicated`) — handling missing lab results.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs (with Exhaustive Code Derivations)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Note:** KTU frequently asks for **output prediction** and **code completion** questions on the operations below. Every line of code in this section is annotated with the *exact transformation logic* it performs on a sample healthcare dataset.

## 3.1 Setup: Building a Sample EHR DataFrame

We will work on a single running example throughout this section — a synthetic Electronic Health Record (EHR) of 5 patients.

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Step 1: Create the raw data as a Python dictionary.
# Each key becomes a column name; each value is a list (column data).
data = {
    'PatientID': ['P001', 'P002', 'P003', 'P004', 'P005'],
    'Name'     : ['Anil', 'Beena', 'Chitra', 'David', 'Esha'],
    'Age'      : [45, 62, 29, 70, 55],
    'BP'       : [120, 145, 110, 160, 130],
    'Glucose'  : [95, 180, 88, 210, 140],
    'Diabetic' : [False, True, False, True, True]
}

# Step 2: Construct the DataFrame.
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Step 3: Optionally set a meaningful index.
df = df.set_index('PatientID')

print(df)
```

**Output (line-by-line derivation):**

```
         Name  Age   BP  Glucose  Diabetic
PatientID
P001      Anil   45  120       95     False
P002     Beena   62  145      180      True
P003   Chitra   29  110       88     False
P004     David   70  160      210      True
P005      Esha   55  130      140      True
```

**Logic:** Dictionary keys → column names. Dictionary values → column data (top-to-bottom). After `set_index`, the `PatientID` column becomes the row index, allowing label-based lookup.

## 3.2 Derivation Walkthrough 1 — Column Selection (`df['col']`)

**Question:** Extract the `Glucose` column.

```python
g = df['Glucose']
print(type(g))      # <class 'pandas.core.series.Series'>
print(g)
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. `df['Glucose']` → selects the column whose label is `'Glucose'`.
2. Pandas looks up this label along **Axis 1** (the columns axis).
3. Returns a **Series** indexed by the DataFrame's index (`PatientID`).
4. The dtype of the returned Series is `int64` because all values are integers.

**Output:**

```
PatientID
P001     95
P002    180
P003     88
P004    210
P005    140
Name: Glucose, dtype: int64
```

## 3.3 Derivation Walkthrough 2 — Boolean Masking (Filtering)

**Question:** Find all diabetic patients older than 50.

```python
mask = (df['Diabetic'] == True) & (df['Age'] > 50)
result = df[mask]
print(result)
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. `(df['Diabetic'] == True)` → element-wise comparison → Series of booleans `[F, T, F, T, T]`.
2. `(df['Age'] > 50)` → element-wise comparison → Series `[F, T, F, T, T]`.
3. Bitwise `&` (NOT `and`) → element-wise AND → Series `[F, T, F, T, T]`.
4. `df[mask]` → keeps only rows where mask is `True` → 3 rows returned.

**Output:**

```
         Name  Age   BP  Glucose  Diabetic
PatientID
P002     Beena   62  145      180      True
P004     David   70  160      210      True
P005      Esha   55  130      140      True
```

## 3.4 Derivation Walkthrough 3 — `loc` vs `iloc`

```python
# Label-based: 'P002' is a row LABEL.
print(df.loc['P002', 'BP'])       # 145

# Position-based: row index 1 (0-based), column index 1.
print(df.iloc[1, 1])              # 62   (Age of Beena)

# Slicing using labels (INCLUSIVE on both ends).
print(df.loc['P001':'P003', ['Name','Glucose']])
```

**Output of slicing:**

```
         Name  Glucose
PatientID
P001      Anil       95
P002     Beena      180
P003   Chitra       88
```

**Logic distinction:**
- `loc` → uses **labels**; slicing `P001:P003` is **inclusive of both ends**.
- `iloc` → uses **integer positions**; slicing `0:3` is **exclusive of the end (Python convention)**.

## 3.5 Derivation Walkthrough 4 — Adding a Computed Column

**Question:** Compute `BMI_Category` based on `Glucose` (≥140 → High, else Normal).

```python
df['Status'] = np.where(df['Glucose'] >= 140, 'High', 'Normal')
print(df)
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. `df['Glucose'] >= 140` → boolean Series `[F, T, F, T, T]`.
2. `np.where(cond, 'High', 'Normal')` → element-wise conditional → Series of strings.
3. Assignment creates a **new column** `'Status'` in the DataFrame.

**Output:**

```
         Name  Age   BP  Glucose  Diabetic  Status
PatientID
P001      Anil   45  120       95     False  Normal
P002     Beena   62  145      180      True    High
P003   Chitra   29  110       88     False  Normal
P004     David   70  160      210      True    High
P005      Esha   55  130      140      True    High
```

## 3.6 Derivation Walkthrough 5 — `groupby` Aggregation

```python
# Average BP and Glucose of diabetic vs non-diabetic patients.
agg = df.groupby('Diabetic')[['BP','Glucose']].mean()
print(agg)
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. `groupby('Diabetic')` → splits the DataFrame into 2 groups (`False`, `True`).
2. `[['BP','Glucose']]` → restricts the analysis to these two numeric columns.
3. `.mean()` → applies the mean function to each group.
4. The result is a new DataFrame indexed by the unique values of `Diabetic`.

**Output:**

```
                 BP     Glucose
Diabetic
False     115.000000   91.500000
True      145.000000  176.666667
```

**Mathematics behind the mean (True group):**

$$
\overline{BP}_{\text{True}} = \frac{145 + 160 + 130}{3} = \frac{435}{3} = 145.0
$$

$$
\overline{Glucose}_{\text{True}} = \frac{180 + 210 + 140}{3} = \frac{530}{3} = 176.67
$$

## 3.7 Derivation Walkthrough 6 — Handling Missing Data

```python
df2 = df.copy()
df2.loc['P003', 'BP'] = np.nan
print(df2.isnull().sum())
print(df2['BP'].fillna(df2['BP'].mean()))
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. `df2.loc['P003','BP'] = np.nan` → introduces a missing value.
2. `df2.isnull()` → element-wise boolean DataFrame.
3. `.sum()` → counts `True` per column → Series showing 1 missing in `BP`.
4. `fillna(mean)` → imputes the missing BP with the column mean.

## 3.8 Derivation Walkthrough 7 — `merge` (SQL-style Join)

```python
billing = pd.DataFrame({
    'PatientID' : ['P001','P002','P004'],
    'BillAmount': [2500, 4800, 6200]
}).set_index('PatientID')

joined = df.merge(billing, on='PatientID', how='left')
print(joined[['Name','BillAmount']])
```

**Logic:** A **LEFT JOIN** keeps *all* rows of `df`; missing matches (`P003`, `P005`) produce `NaN` in `BillAmount`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DataFrame

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[RAW DATA SOURCES] --> B[DATA INGESTION LAYER]
    B --> B1[CSV read_csv]
    B --> B2[SQL read_sql]
    B --> B3[Dict DataFrame]
    B1 --> C[IN-MEMORY DATAFRAME OBJECT]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[AXIS 0 ROW INDEX PATIENT ID]
    C --> E[AXIS 1 COLUMN LABELS VITALS]
    C --> F[DTYPE MATRIX INT FLOAT OBJECT]
    C --> G[OPERATION LAYER]
    G --> G1[SELECTION loc iloc]
    G --> G2[FILTERING BOOLEAN MASK]
    G --> G3[TRANSFORMATION APPLY ASSIGN]
    G --> G4[AGGREGATION GROUPBY PIVOT]
    G --> G5[RESTRUCTURING MERGE CONCAT]
    G1 --> H[CLEAN TIDY DATA]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I[ANALYTICS VISUALIZATION ML READY]
```

## 4.2 DataFrame Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NEED TO ACCESS DATA] --> B{KNOWN BY LABEL OR POSITION}
    B -->|LABEL| C[USE loc AT OR QUERY]
    B -->|POSITION| D[USE iloc IAT]
    B -->|SINGLE VALUE FAST| E[AT OR IAT]
    C --> F[RETURNS SERIES OR DATAFRAME]
    D --> F
    E --> G[RETURNS SCALAR]
    A --> H[NEED FILTERING]
    H --> I[BOOLEAN MASK]
    I --> J[df mask CONDITION]
```

## 4.3 Split-Apply-Combine Pattern of `groupby`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FULL DATAFRAME 5 PATIENTS] --> B[SPLIT BY DIABETIC]
    B --> B1[GROUP FALSE 2 PATIENTS]
    B --> B2[GROUP TRUE 3 PATIENTS]
    B1 --> C1[APPLY MEAN]
    B2 --> C2[APPLY MEAN]
    C1 --> D1[AGGREGATE BP 115 GLUC 91.5]
    C2 --> D2[AGGREGATE BP 145 GLUC 176.67]
    D1 --> E[RESULT DATAFRAME INDEXED BY DIABETIC]
    D2 --> E
```

## 4.4 Memory Map of a DataFrame

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COLUMNS_AXIS
        C0[Name OBJECT]
        C1[Age INT64]
        C2[BP INT64]
        C3[Glucose INT64]
        C4[Diabetic BOOL]
    end
    subgraph INDEX_AXIS
        I0[P001]
        I1[P002]
        I2[P003]
        I3[P004]
        I4[P005]
    end
    I0 --> C0
    I0 --> C1
    I0 --> C2
    I0 --> C3
    I0 --> C4
    I1 --> C0
    I1 --> C1
    I1 --> C2
    I1 --> C3
    I1 --> C4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Using `and` / `or` instead of `&` / `|`** inside boolean masks. Python's `and` is not vectorized — it raises `ValueError: The truth value of a Series is ambiguous`. Always use bitwise operators with parentheses.
> 2. **Confusing `loc` (label-based) and `iloc` (position-based).** After `set_index`, row positions change but labels remain. A common error: `df.loc[0]` fails if the index is `'P001'…`.
> 3. **Forgetting `inplace=True` or assignment.** `df.drop(...)` returns a *new* DataFrame; the original is unchanged unless `inplace=True` is set.
> 4. **Ignoring `SettingWithCopyWarning`.** Always use `df.loc[mask, 'col'] = value` instead of chained assignment.
> 5. **Confusing `df['col']` (returns Series) with `df[['col']]` (returns DataFrame).** This distinction is tested in 3-mark questions.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**Differentiate between `loc` and `iloc` accessor in Pandas with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `loc` | `iloc` |
|---|---|---|
| Selection basis | **Label-based** | **Integer-position-based** |
| End of slice | **Inclusive** | **Exclusive** (Python convention) |
| Boolean array | Allowed (must align with index) | Allowed (must align with positions) |
| Example | `df.loc['P002', 'BP']` | `df.iloc[1, 1]` |
| Use when | You know the *name* of the row/column | You know the *numeric position* |

```python
# loc
df.loc['P002', 'BP']    # 145  (Beena's BP)
# iloc
df.iloc[1, 1]           # 62   (row position 1, col position 1)
```

**(1 mark)** for definition of each, **(1 mark)** for distinguishing feature (inclusive vs exclusive), and **(1 mark)** for the example.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**Explain the split-apply-combine strategy of `groupby()` in Pandas.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Split (1 mark):** The DataFrame is divided into groups based on the unique values of the key column (e.g., `Diabetic` → `True`, `False`).
2. **Apply (1 mark):** A function (mean, sum, count, etc.) is applied independently to each group.
3. **Combine (1 mark):** The results from all groups are merged back into a single DataFrame indexed by the group keys.

**Example:**
```python
df.groupby('Diabetic')['BP'].mean()
```
This *splits* the 5 patients into 2 groups, *applies* `mean()` to `BP` in each, and *combines* into a 2-row Series.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following patient dataset:

```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'PID'  : [101,102,103,104,105,106],
    'Name' : ['A','B','C','D','E','F'],
    'Age'  : [34, 67, 45, 70, 29, 55],
    'BP'   : [118, 150, 125, 165, 110, 140],
    'Sugar': [90, 170, 100, 200, 85, 160]
})
```

Write Python code (using Pandas) to perform the following operations and write the resulting output:
1. Display the first 3 rows.
2. Select only the `Name` and `BP` columns.
3. Filter patients whose `Age > 50` **and** `BP > 140`.
4. Compute the mean of the `Sugar` column.
5. Sort the DataFrame in descending order of `Age`.
6. Add a new column `Risk` = `'High'` if `Sugar >= 140`, else `'Low'`.
7. Count the number of patients in each `Risk` category.

**Model Solution:**

```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'PID'  : [101,102,103,104,105,106],
    'Name' : ['A','B','C','D','E','F'],
    'Age'  : [34, 67, 45, 70, 29, 55],
    'BP'   : [118, 150, 125, 165, 110, 140],
    'Sugar': [90, 170, 100, 200, 85, 160]
})

# (1) First 3 rows
print(df.head(3))
#    PID Name  Age   BP  Sugar
# 0  101    A   34  118     90
# 1  102    B   67  150    170
# 2  103    C   45  125    100

# (2) Select Name and BP columns
print(df[['Name','BP']])

# (3) Filter Age > 50 AND BP > 140
print(df[(df['Age']>50) & (df['BP']>140)])
#    PID Name  Age   BP  Sugar
# 1  102    B   67  150    170
# 3  104    D   70  165    200
# 5  106    F   55  140    160

# (4) Mean of Sugar
print(df['Sugar'].mean())     # 134.1666...

# (5) Sort by Age descending
print(df.sort_values('Age', ascending=False))

# (6) New column Risk
df['Risk'] = (df['Sugar']>=140).map({True:'High', False:'Low'})

# (7) Count of each Risk
print(df['Risk'].value_counts())
# High    3
# Low     3
```

**Mark split:** (1)→1m, (2)→1m, (3)→2m (correct use of `&` + parentheses), (4)→0.5m, (5)→0.5m, (6)→1m, (7)→1m.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of `merge()` joins in Pandas with examples. How is `merge()` different from `concat()`?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

`pd.merge(df1, df2, on='key', how='<type>')` supports four join types, identical to SQL:

| Join Type | Description | Keeps Rows from |
|---|---|---|
| `inner` (default) | Intersection of keys | Only matching rows in **both** |
| `left` | All rows of left + matched from right | All of left |
| `right` | All rows of right + matched from left | All of right |
| `outer` | Union of keys | All rows from both; `NaN` for no match |

**Code example:**
```python
patients  = pd.DataFrame({'PID':[1,2,3], 'Name':['A','B','C']})
bills     = pd.DataFrame({'PID':[1,2,4], 'Bill':[500,800,700]})

pd.merge(patients, bills, on='PID', how='inner')   # PIDs 1,2 only
pd.merge(patients, bills, on='PID', how='left')    # PIDs 1,2,3 (Bill=NaN for 3)
pd.merge(patients, bills, on='PID', how='right')   # PIDs 1,2,4 (Name=NaN for 4)
pd.merge(patients, bills, on='PID', how='outer')   # PIDs 1,2,3,4 (NaN where unmatched)
```

**Difference from `concat()` (2 marks):**
- `merge()` joins on a **common key column** (SQL-style).
- `concat()` simply **stacks** DataFrames along an axis (row-wise or column-wise) and does *not* perform key-based matching.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram/description, explain the internal structure of a Pandas DataFrame. How does it differ from a NumPy 2D array?

**Model Answer:**
A DataFrame has **three components**:
1. **Index** — a sequence of row labels (Axis 0).
2. **Columns** — a sequence of column labels (Axis 1).
3. **Data block** — a NumPy 2D array (or a dict of Series) holding the actual values.

Each *column* in a DataFrame is internally a **Pandas Series** that shares a common index. This is why column-wise operations (e.g., `df['BP'].mean()`) are first-class.

**Differences from a NumPy 2D array (3 marks):**
- NumPy arrays are **homogeneous** (single dtype); DataFrame columns can be **heterogeneous**.
- NumPy uses integer positions only; DataFrame supports **label-based** indexing.
- DataFrame has **built-in handling for missing data (`NaN`)** and **time-series** operations.
- DataFrame supports **metadata** (column names, index names).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given a DataFrame `df` with columns `Department`, `Doctor`, `Patients_Seen`, and `Revenue`, write the code to:
1. Find the total revenue per department.
2. Find the top-earning doctor overall.
3. Create a pivot table of average `Patients_Seen` indexed by `Department` and columns by `Doctor`.
4. Replace any missing `Revenue` with the department-wise mean.

**Model Solution:**
```python
# (1) Total revenue per department
print(df.groupby('Department')['Revenue'].sum())

# (2) Top-earning doctor (sum across departments)
doc_rev = df.groupby('Doctor')['Revenue'].sum()
print(doc_rev.idxmax(), doc_rev.max())

# (3) Pivot table
print(df.pivot_table(values='Patients_Seen',
                     index='Department',
                     columns='Doctor',
                     aggfunc='mean'))

# (4) Impute missing Revenue with department mean
df['Revenue'] = df.groupby('Department')['Revenue']\
                  .transform(lambda x: x.fillna(x.mean()))
```

**Mark split:** (1)→1.5m, (2)→1.5m, (3)→2m, (4)→2m.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin This for Last-Night Revision**

- A **DataFrame** is a **2D labeled table** with **row index (Axis 0)** and **column labels (Axis 1)**.
- **Creation:** `pd.DataFrame(dict)`, `pd.read_csv()`, `pd.read_sql()`.
- **Inspection:** `head`, `tail`, `info`, `describe`, `shape`, `dtypes`, `columns`, `index`.
- **Selection — Three Pillars:**
  - `df['col']` → Series (single column).
  - `df[['c1','c2']]` → DataFrame (multiple columns).
  - `df.loc[rows, cols]` → label-based; `df.iloc[rows, cols]` → position-based.
- **Filtering:** Boolean masking **requires** bitwise `&` / `|` with **parentheses**.
- **Modification:** Use `df.loc[mask, 'col'] = value` to avoid `SettingWithCopyWarning`.
- **Sorting:** `sort_values(by='col', ascending=...)`; `sort_index()`.
- **Aggregation:** `mean`, `sum`, `median`, `std`, `min`, `max`, `count`, `agg`.
- **GroupBy:** Split → Apply → Combine. Always chain with an aggregation function.
- **Pivot Table:** `pivot_table(values, index, columns, aggfunc='mean')`.
