# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Deep Learning Pipeline in Healthcare

The end-to-end workflow of a deep learning system in a clinical setting follows these structured steps:

1. **Data Acquisition** — Collect medical data from PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System), EHRs, wearables, and laboratory systems.
2. **Data Preprocessing** — Standardize formats (DICOM → PNG/NIfTI), normalize pixel intensities, handle missing values, and remove Protected Health Information (PHI) for de-identification.
3. **Data Augmentation** — Apply rotations, flips, zoom, and elastic deformations to artificially expand the training set (critical in medical imaging where data is scarce).
4. **Model Selection** — Choose architecture based on data type: CNN for images, RNN/LSTM for sequences, Transformer for text.
5. **Training** — Feed labeled data through the network, compute loss, and update weights using backpropagation + optimizer (e.g., Adam, SGD).
6. **Validation** — Tune hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, dropout) on a held-out validation set.
7. **Testing** — Evaluate final performance on unseen test data using clinical metrics (AUROC, Sensitivity, Specificity).
8. **Deployment & Monitoring** — Integrate into hospital PACS/CDS (Clinical Decision Support) system; continuously monitor for *model drift*.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation of a Neural Network

### 2.2.1 The Artificial Neuron (Perceptron)

A single neuron performs two operations: a **linear combination** of inputs followed by a **non-linear activation**.

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

$$a = \sigma(z)$$

where $\mathbf{x}$ is the input vector, $\mathbf{w}$ is the weight vector, $b$ is the bias term, and $\sigma$ is the activation function.

### 2.2.2 Common Activation Functions

| Activation | Formula | Derivative | Use Case in Healthcare |
|------------|---------|------------|------------------------|
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | $\sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ | Binary classification (benign/malignant) output layer |
| ReLU | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | $0$ if $z < 0$, else $1$ | Hidden layers of CNNs (default choice) |
| Tanh | $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$ | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ | LSTM cell state gating |
| Softmax | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | — | Multi-class classification (disease stage 1/2/3/4) |

### 2.2.3 Forward Propagation in a Multi-Layer Network

For an $L$-layer network, the forward pass recursively computes:

$$\mathbf{a}^{[l]} = \sigma^{[l]}(\mathbf{W}^{[l]} \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}) \quad \text{for } l = 1, 2, \ldots, L$$

where $\mathbf{a}^{[0]} = \mathbf{x}$ is the input, and $\mathbf{a}^{[L]}$ is the final prediction $\hat{y}$.

### 2.2.4 Loss Functions

| Task Type | Loss Function | Formula |
|-----------|---------------|---------|
| Binary Classification | Binary Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L} = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$ |
| Multi-Class Classification | Categorical Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L} = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$ |
| Regression (e.g., predicting BP) | Mean Squared Error | $\mathcal{L} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}_i - y_i)^2$ |
| Segmentation (U-Net) | Dice Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{dice} = 1 - \dfrac{2 \sum \hat{y}_i y_i + \epsilon}{\sum \hat{y}_i^2 + \sum y_i^2 + \epsilon}$ |

### 2.2.5 Backpropagation — The Heart of Learning

Backpropagation applies the **chain rule of calculus** to compute gradients of the loss with respect to every weight, enabling gradient descent.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{[L]}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{[L]}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[L]}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[L]}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{[L-1]}} \cdots \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[l]}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}}$$

### 2.2.6 Weight Update Rule (Gradient Descent)

$$\mathbf{W}^{[l]} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{[l]} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}}$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (a hyperparameter typically in the range **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$**).

## 2.3 Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for Medical Imaging

CNNs are the **backbone of modern medical image analysis** because they exploit the **spatial locality** and **translation invariance** inherent in scans.

### 2.3.1 The Convolution Operation

A 2D convolution slides a small **kernel (filter)** $\mathbf{K}$ of size $k \times k$ across an input image $\mathbf{X}$ to produce a **feature map** $\mathbf{S}$:

$$S(i, j) = (\mathbf{X} * \mathbf{K})(i, j) = \sum_{m=0}^{k-1}\sum_{n=0}^{k-1} X(i+m, j+n) \cdot K(m, n) + b$$

### 2.3.2 Pooling Operation (Down-sampling)

**Max Pooling** with a $2 \times 2$ window and stride 2:

$$P(i, j) = \max_{(m,n) \in \text{window}} X(i+m, j+n)$$

This reduces spatial dimensions by a factor of 2, lowering computational cost and providing **local translation invariance**.

### 2.3.3 Receptive Field

The **receptive field** of a neuron in layer $l$ is the region of the input image that influences its activation. Stacking multiple convolutions and poolings expands the receptive field, enabling the network to capture **global context** (e.g., an entire lung lobe) from local features (e.g., a nodule edge).

## 2.4 Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) and LSTM for Sequential Health Data

EHR data, ECG signals, and ICU monitoring streams are **temporal sequences**. RNNs maintain a **hidden state** that summarizes past information.

### 2.4.1 Vanilla RNN Forward Pass

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh} \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$$

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{W}_{hy} \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y$$

**Problem:** Vanilla RNNs suffer from the **vanishing/exploding gradient problem**, making it hard to learn long-range dependencies (e.g., a medication prescribed 6 months ago affecting today's outcome).

### 2.4.2 LSTM Cell — The Solution

LSTMs introduce a **memory cell** $\mathbf{C}_t$ and three **gates** (forget, input, output) to selectively retain or discard information.

**Forget Gate** (decides what to discard from cell state):

$$\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_f \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f\right)$$

**Input Gate** (decides what new information to store):

$$\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_i \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i\right)$$

**Candidate Cell State** (new candidate values):

$$\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh\left(\mathbf{W}_C \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C\right)$$

**Cell State Update** (forget old, add new):

$$\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$$

**Output Gate** (decides what to output from cell state):

$$\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_o \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o\right)$$

**Hidden State Output:**

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Healthcare Example:** In sepsis prediction, the LSTM reads the patient's vitals (heart rate, BP, temperature) every hour. The forget gate may "forget" normal fluctuations, the input gate stores the onset of a fever pattern, and the output gate fires an early warning when the cell state crosses a critical threshold.

## 2.5 Autoencoders for Anomaly Detection

An **autoencoder** compresses input $\mathbf{x}$ into a low-dimensional latent representation $\mathbf{z}$ and reconstructs $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$. The reconstruction error $\vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \vert^2$ flags anomalies.

$$\mathbf{z} = \sigma_{\text{enc}}(\mathbf{W}_e \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_e) \quad \text{(Encoder)}$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \sigma_{\text{dec}}(\mathbf{W}_d \mathbf{z} + \mathbf{b}_d) \quad \text{(Decoder)}$$

## 2.6 U-Net for Medical Image Segmentation

**U-Net** is the gold-standard architecture for medical image segmentation (e.g., delineating tumor boundaries in brain MRI). It has a symmetric **encoder-decoder** structure with **skip connections** that preserve fine-grained spatial detail.

**Loss:** Combination of Dice Loss + Binary Cross-Entropy:

$$\mathcal{L}_{U\text{-Net}} = \mathcal{L}_{dice} + \mathcal{L}_{BCE}$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Neuron output | $a = \sigma\left(\sum_{i} w_i x_i + b\right)$ | Single unit computation |
| 2 | Forward pass | $\mathbf{a}^{[l]} = \sigma^{[l]}\left(\mathbf{W}^{[l]} \mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}\right)$ | Layer-wise activation |
| 3 | Binary cross-entropy | $\mathcal{L} = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum \left[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\right]$ | Classification loss |
| 4 | MSE loss | $\mathcal{L} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum (\hat{y} - y)^2$ | Regression loss |
| 5 | Dice loss | $\mathcal{L} = 1 - \dfrac{2\sum \hat{y} y + \epsilon}{\sum \hat{y}^2 + \sum y^2 + \epsilon}$ | Segmentation loss |
| 6 | Convolution | $S(i,j) = \sum_m \sum_n X(i+m,j+n) \cdot K(m,n) + b$ | Feature extraction |
| 7 | Max pooling | $P(i,j) = \max_{m,n} X(i+m, j+n)$ | Down-sampling |
| 8 | ReLU | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Non-linearity |
| 9 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Probability output |
| 10 | Softmax | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class probability |
| 11 | Gradient update | $\mathbf{W} \leftarrow \mathbf{W} - \eta \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}}$ | Weight optimization |
| 12 | LSTM forget gate | $\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_f[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f\right)$ | Memory control |
| 13 | LSTM cell update | $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ | Long-term memory |
| 14 | Sensitivity | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Recall (true positive rate) |
| 15 | Specificity | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True negative rate |
| 16 | F1-Score | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balanced metric |
| 17 | AUROC | Area under ROC curve | Discrimination ability |
| 18 | Dice score | $\dfrac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Segmentation overlap |
| 19 | Receptive field expansion | $r_{l} = r_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{l-1} s_j$ | Contextual reach |
| 20 | Autoencoder reconstruction | $\mathcal{L}_{AE} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum \vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \vert^2$ | Anomaly detection loss |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Deep learning in healthcare is not theoretical — it powers **FDA-cleared products** in active clinical use:

- **IDx-DR (Digital Diagnostic):** First FDA-authorized autonomous AI diagnostic system — detects diabetic retinopathy from retinal fundus photographs.
- **Viz.ai (LVO Detection):** CNN-based CT scan analysis that alerts neuro-interventionalists within minutes of detecting a large vessel occlusion stroke.
- **Paige.AI (Pathology):** Deep learning model for prostate cancer detection in biopsy slides.
- **DeepMind's AlphaFold:** Predicts 3D protein structures — accelerates drug discovery for rare diseases.
- **Epic Sepsis Model:** LSTM-based early warning system deployed in hospitals to predict sepsis 6 hours before clinical onset.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Forward & Backward Pass on a 2-Layer Network

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
Given a binary classification task (malignant vs. benign tumor), we have:
- Input: $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$, true label $y = 1$
- Hidden layer weights: $\mathbf{W}^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b}^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$
- Output layer weights: $\mathbf{W}^{[2]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b}^{[2]} = 0.2$
- Activation: ReLU in hidden layer, Sigmoid in output layer
- Learning rate: $\eta = 0.1$

### Step 1: Forward Pass — Hidden Layer (Layer 1)

Compute the linear combination $\mathbf{z}^{[1]}$:

$$\mathbf{z}^{[1]} = \mathbf{W}^{[1]} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{[1]}$$

$$\mathbf{z}^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Row 1 calculation:**
$$z_1^{[1]} = (0.1 \times 1.0) + (0.2 \times 0.5) + 0.1 = 0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1 = 0.3$$

**Row 2 calculation:**
$$z_2^{[1]} = (0.3 \times 1.0) + (0.4 \times 0.5) + 0.1 = 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 0.6$$

$$\mathbf{z}^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply ReLU activation $\mathbf{a}^{[1]} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{z}^{[1]})$:

$$\mathbf{a}^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} \max(0, 0.3) \\ \max(0, 0.6) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Forward Pass — Output Layer (Layer 2)

$$z^{[2]} = \mathbf{W}^{[2]} \mathbf{a}^{[1]} + b^{[2]}$$

$$z^{[2]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} + 0.2$$

$$z^{[2]} = (0.5 \times 0.3) + (0.6 \times 0.6) + 0.2 = 0.15 + 0.36 + 0.2 = 0.71$$

Apply Sigmoid activation:
$$\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{[2]}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.71}}$$

$$e^{-0.71} \approx 0.4916$$

$$\hat{y} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.4916} = \frac{1}{1.4916} \approx 0.6704$$

### Step 3: Compute the Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy)

$$\mathcal{L} = -\left[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1 - y)\log(1 - \hat{y})\right]$$

$$\mathcal{L} = -\left[1 \cdot \log(0.6704) + 0 \cdot \log(0.3296)\right]$$

$$\mathcal{L} = -\log(0.6704) = -(-0.4001) = 0.4001$$

### Step 4: Backward Pass — Compute Gradients

**Step 4a: Gradient of Loss w.r.t. $\hat{y}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\frac{y}{\hat{y}} + \frac{1 - y}{1 - \hat{y}} = -\frac{1}{0.6704} + 0 \approx -1.4917$$

**Step 4b: Gradient of Loss w.r.t. $z^{[2]}$** (using chain rule with sigmoid derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{[2]}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \sigma'(z^{[2]}) = -1.4917 \times (0.6704 \times 0.3296)$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{[2]}} = -1.4917 \times 0.2210 \approx -0.3297$$

*(This is the well-known simplification: for sigmoid + BCE, the gradient is simply $\hat{y} - y$.)*

**Step 4c: Gradients w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}^{[2]}$ and $b^{[2]}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[2]}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{[2]}} \cdot \mathbf{a}^{[1] T} = -0.3297 \times \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0989 \\ -0.1978 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{[2]}} = -0.3297$$

**Step 4d: Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{a}^{[1]}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{[1]}} = \mathbf{W}^{[2] T} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{[2]}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} \times (-0.3297) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1649 \\ -0.1978 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4e: Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{z}^{[1]}$** (apply ReLU derivative: 1 if $z > 0$, else 0):

Since both $z_1^{[1]} = 0.3 > 0$ and $z_2^{[1]} = 0.6 > 0$, ReLU derivative is 1 for both:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[1]}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{[1]}} \odot \text{ReLU}'(\mathbf{z}^{[1]}) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1649 \\ -0.1978 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4f: Gradients w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}^{[1]}$ and $\mathbf{b}^{[1]}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[1]}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{[1]}} \cdot \mathbf{x}^T = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1649 \\ -0.1978 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[1]}} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1649 & -0.0825 \\ -0.1978 & -0.0989 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{[1]}} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1649 \\ -0.1978 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Weight Update (Gradient Descent with $\eta = 0.1$)

**Update $\mathbf{W}^{[2]}$:**
$$\mathbf{W}^{[2]}_{new} = \mathbf{W}^{[2]} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[2]}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \times \begin{bmatrix} -0.0989 \\ -0.1978 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{W}^{[2]}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 + 0.00989 \\ 0.6 + 0.01978 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5099 \\ 0.6198 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Update $b^{[2]}$:**
$$b^{[2]}_{new} = 0.2 - 0.1 \times (-0.3297) = 0.2 + 0.03297 = 0.2330$$

**Update $\mathbf{W}^{[1]}$:**
$$\mathbf{W}^{[1]}_{new} = \mathbf{W}^{[1]} - 0.1 \times \begin{bmatrix} -0.1649 & -0.0825 \\ -0.1978 & -0.0989 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{W}^{[1]}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 + 0.01649 & 0.2 + 0.00825 \\ 0.3 + 0.01978 & 0.4 + 0.00989 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1165 & 0.2082 \\ 0.3198 & 0.4099 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Update $\mathbf{b}^{[1]}$:**
$$\mathbf{b}^{[1]}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 + 0.01649 \\ 0.1 + 0.01978 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1165 \\ 0.1198 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that *all* weights were updated to *increase* the predicted probability $\hat{y}$ toward 1 (the true label). The model has learned from a single example. Repeating this process over millions of labeled medical images produces a highly accurate diagnostic network.

## 3.2 Worked Example: CNN Convolution on a 5×5 Chest X-ray Patch

**Problem (KTU-style):** Given a 5×5 grayscale patch from a chest X-ray and a 3×3 edge-detection kernel, compute the feature map (no padding, stride = 1).

**Input Image $X$:**
$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 10 & 10 & 10 & 10 \\ 10 & 10 & 10 & 10 & 10 \\ 10 & 10 & 50 & 10 & 10 \\ 10 & 10 & 10 & 10 & 10 \\ 10 & 10 & 10 & 10 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Kernel $K$ (vertical edge detector):**
$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b = 0$$

### Output Feature Map Calculation

**Output size formula:** $\text{Output size} = (W - K + 1) = (5 - 3 + 1) = 3$, so feature map is $3 \times 3$.

**Position (0,0):** Sum element-wise product of $X[0:3, 0:3]$ and $K$:
$$S(0,0) = (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1)$$
$$S(0,0) = 10 + 0 - 10 + 10 + 0 - 10 + 10 + 0 - 10 = 0$$

**Position (0,1):** Use window $X[0:3, 1:4]$:
$$S(0,1) = (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (50 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1)$$
$$S(0,1) = 10 + 0 - 10 + 10 + 0 - 10 + 10 + 0 - 10 = 0$$

**Position (0,2):** Use window $X[0:3, 2:5]$:
$$S(0,2) = (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (50 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) = 0$$

**Position (1,0):** Use window $X[1:4, 0:3]$:
$$S(1,0) = (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (50 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1)$$
$$S(1,0) = 10 + 0 - 10 + 10 + 0 - 50 + 10 + 0 - 10 = -40$$

**Position (1,1):** Use window $X[1:4, 1:4]$:
$$S(1,1) = (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (50 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1)$$
$$S(1,1) = 10 + 0 - 10 + 10 + 0 - 10 + 10 + 0 - 10 = 0$$

**Position (1,2):** Use window $X[1:4, 2:5]$:
$$S(1,2) = (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (50 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1)$$
$$S(1,2) = 10 + 0 - 10 + 50 + 0 - 10 + 10 + 0 - 10 = +40$$

**Position (2,0):** Use window $X[2:5, 0:3]$ — all 10s:
$$S(2,0) = 10 - 10 + 10 - 10 + 10 - 10 + 10 - 10 + 10 = 0$$

**Position (2,1):** Use window $X[2:5, 1:4]$:
$$S(2,1) = (10 \cdot 1) + (50 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) + (10 \cdot 1) + (10 \cdot 0) + (10 \cdot -1) = 0$$

**Position (2,2):** Use window $X[2:5, 2:5]$ — all 10s:
$$S(2,2) = 0$$

**Final Feature Map:**
$$S = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ -40 & 0 & +40 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Healthcare Interpretation:** The kernel detected a **strong horizontal contrast** at row 1 (bright value 50 against dark 10s), producing activations of $-40$ (left side) and $+40$ (right side). In a real chest X-ray, this corresponds to detecting a **sharp tissue-density transition** — a hallmark of a nodule or mass boundary. Stacking 64 such kernels in a CNN layer allows simultaneous detection of many different anatomical structures.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right DL Architecture

| Data Type | Recommended Architecture | Reason | Clinical Example |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------|------------------|
| 2D Image (X-ray, CT slice) | **CNN (ResNet, DenseNet)** | Spatial locality, hierarchical features | Pneumonia detection |
| 3D Volumetric Scan (MRI, CT volume) | **3D CNN (3D U-Net)** | Volumetric context | Brain tumor segmentation |
| Time Series (ECG, EEG) | **1D CNN + LSTM** | Local patterns + temporal dependencies | Arrhythmia detection |
| Sequential EHR codes | **LSTM / GRU** | Long-range temporal dependencies | Sepsis prediction |
| Clinical text (notes) | **Transformer / BERT** | Bidirectional context understanding | Radiology report summarization |
| Tabular EHR (vitals, labs) | **MLP / TabNet** | Mixed feature types | 30-day readmission risk |
| Image + Text (multi-modal) | **CNN + Transformer fusion** | Joint reasoning | Visual question answering in radiology |
| Rare disease data | **Autoencoder + Anomaly Detection** | Unsupervised learning | Novel pathogen detection |

## 4.5 Transfer Learning Workflow for Limited Medical Data

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Large General Dataset ImageNet] --> B[Pre-trained CNN ResNet50]
    B --> C[Freeze Early Layers]
    C --> D[Replace Final Layers]
    D --> E[Fine-tune on Medical Data]
    E --> F[Chest X-ray Dataset]
    F --> G[Trained Diagnostic Model]
    G --> H[Deploy in Hospital PACS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Transfer Learning Matters in Healthcare:** Medical datasets are often tiny (hundreds of CT scans vs. millions of ImageNet photos). Pre-training on large natural image datasets and fine-tuning on medical images leverages generic visual features (edges, textures) to overcome data scarcity — a standard KTU discussion point.
